coredata.dc:title: Role of carbon quantum dots and film thickness on enhanced UV shielding capability of flexible polymer film containing carbon quantum dots/N-doped ZnO nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Uthirakumar, Periyayya
Devendiran, M.
Yun, Jin-Hyeon
Kim, Gyu Cheol
Kalaiarasan, S.
Lee, In-Hwan
coredata.dc:description: We report a simple method for preparing flexible polymer film containing carbon quantum dots/N-doped ZnO (CQD/N-ZnO) nanoparticles, as UV shielding material. The polymer film showed an enhanced UV shielding capability with increasing of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles. The maximum of 47% of UV shielding capability was achieved with 1 wt.% of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles and two-order enhancement was achieved by controlling film thickness to 250 μm. It was found that CQDs plays important role to promote compatibility of hydrophilic ZnO into hydrophobic polymer matrix without disturbing the transparency of the film. An excellent UV shielding capability of 85% and 92% was witnessed in 250 μm thick polymer film under a UV lamp and direct daylight, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Zinc oxide
UV-shielding
Polymer film
Nanoparticles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic representation for the preparation of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles dispersed PMMA polymer film.
XRD patterns of (a) CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles and (b) CQD/N-ZnO dispersed PMMA polymer films.
HRTEM image (a) and particles size analysis (b) of CQDs.

(a) FESEM image, (b) TEM image of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles, HRTEM lattice space values of (c) N-ZnO nanomaterials and (d) CQDs. Inset in Fig. 4b: selected area electron diffraction pattern of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles.
A schematic illustrates the evaluation of UV shielding capability of polymer film with the help of UV digital lux meter.

UV shielding capability of polymer film with respect to different weight percentage of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles under (a) a UV lamp, and (b) daylight sources. Inset: photograph of polymer films with respect to increasing wt% of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles.
UV–vis absorption spectrum of 1.0PCZ polymer film containing 1 wt.% of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles. Inset: photographic images of pristine PMMA film and 250 μm thick polymer film.
UV shielding capability of different thick polymer films under (a) daylight, (b) a UV lamp, and comparative graph of pristine PMMA, N-ZnO and PCZ-4 polymer films.

head.ce:title: Role of carbon quantum dots and film thickness on enhanced UV shielding capability of flexible polymer film containing carbon quantum dots/N-doped ZnO nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Korea University, Seoul, 02841, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanoscience Center for Optoelectronic and Energy Devices, Sona College of Technology, Salem, Tamilnadu, 636005, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanoscience Center for Optoelectronic and Energy Devices

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Nanoscience Center for Optoelectronic and Energy Devices, Sona College of Technology, Salem, Tamilnadu, 636005, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanoscience Center for Optoelectronic and Energy Devices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a simple method for preparing flexible polymer film containing carbon quantum dots/N-doped ZnO (CQD/N-ZnO) nanoparticles, as UV shielding material. The polymer film showed an enhanced UV shielding capability with increasing of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles. The maximum of 47% of UV shielding capability was achieved with 1 wt.% of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles and two-order enhancement was achieved by controlling film thickness to 250 μm. It was found that CQDs plays important role to promote compatibility of hydrophilic ZnO into hydrophobic polymer matrix without disturbing the transparency of the film. An excellent UV shielding capability of 85% and 92% was witnessed in 250 μm thick polymer film under a UV lamp and direct daylight, respectively.

A free-standing and flexible polymer film containing CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles can be explored as potential candidate as UV light filter. The maximum of ∼92% of UV light was shielded by the 250 μm thick polymer film containing just 1.0% of CQD/N-ZnO nanocomposites, under the direct daylight and the results are consistent with UV lamp source.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: In the past few decades, there is a great deal of attention to develop variety of UV-shielding materials. So far, both organic and inorganic UV shielding materials have been developed to block UV radiation [1]. Wang et al. reported UV-shielding property of organic molecules dispersed poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) polymer films that blocked UV light below 300 nm even with a low content of Sepia eumelanin [2]. Further, dopamine-melanin hollow nanoparticles in PVA film established an excellent UV shielding with relatively high transparency to visible light [2,3]. However, in comparison with organic UV shielding materials, inorganic materials are more favorable in practical applications due to their high optical, chemical stability, wide shielding range and low toxicity [4–6]. The dispersion of UV-shielding materials such as ZnO [7,8], CuO [9], CeO2 [10], and TiO2 [11] were reported by making polymer film showing an improved UV-shielding capacity. Among these materials, ZnO nanoparticles have received a promising material by reason of its nontoxicity, low cost, and chemical stability in air [7,8]. Demir et al. discussed ZnO nanoparticles dispersed poly (methylmethacrylate) (PMMA) composite films showed a strong UV absorption and light transmittance in visible region [12]. Chae et al. reported a strong UV-absorbance of ZnO/PS nanocomposite films without losing transparency at low ZnO content [13].

Property of polymer film is certainly influenced by several factors including particle size, degree of dispersion and amount of nanomaterials. In general, it was hard to block entire UV light with lower content of inorganic nanomaterial without proper surface modification due to fewer compatibility. Also, transparency of the polymer film gets reduced drastically because of severe aggregation of UV shielding nanoparticles due to high polarity and large specific surface area [14]. To overcome these issues, a suitable surface modification is necessary to prevent nanoparticles agglomeration, which promotes the compatibility of hydrophilic ZnO in hydrophobic polymer even at higher loading [15]. Recently, long chain poly(ethylene glycol) methyl ether, oleic acid, octylamine, ionic liquids, carbon dots, and silane compound have been identified as surface modifier to effectively dispersed ZnO nanomaterials in the polymeric matrix [16–21]. Mallakpour et al. incorporated citric acid and vitamin C as coupling agents to disperse ZrO2 nanoparticles for UV shielding property [22]. Besides, the carbon dots have received great interest because of their advantages in non-toxic, eco-friendly, photo-stability, and low cost [23]. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has been identified as a surface modifier to improve the compatibility of CuO nanosheets to the PMMA polymer matrix [20]. In continuation to our earlier work, CQDs covered N-ZnO nanoparticles were prepared to improve the compatibility of hydrophilic N-ZnO nanoparticles to the hydrophobic PMMA polymer matrix. The technological application of these types of inorganic materials dispersed polymer films could be used as UV-shielding film to protect UV-light sensitive foods [24], living creatures [25], organic dyes [26] and also useful for preparing sunscreen lotion [27].

In this work, we describe the fabrication of flexible polymer films containing CQDs/N-ZnO nanoparticles. The UV-shielding capability was measured with the polymer films under the illumination of two different light sources, a UV lamp and daylight. The present study could of first report to focus on the preparation of 250 μm thick polymer films showing an excellent UV shielding capacity (∼92%), under the direct daylight.

All the chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. The N-doped ZnO nanoparticles, CQDs, and CQDs covered N-ZnO nanomaterials were prepared as previously reported [28,29]. The detailed synthetic methods were described in supplementary information file.

CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles dispersed polymer films were prepared as a function of increasing the content of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles in the PMMA polymer matrix, based on earlier report [20]. Briefly, 1.0 g of PMMA and 5 mg of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles were taken in a beaker containing 30 ml of acetone. The mixture was stirred for 60 min on a magnetic stirrer for uniform distribution of CQD/N-ZnO in the polymer matrix, as shown in Fig. 1 . The resultant viscose polymer solution was transferred into a stainless steel cup, and it was allowed to stand for 10 h for complete curing. The obtained polymer film containing 0.5 wt% of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles was labelled as 0.5PCZ. Likewise, other polymer films, such as 0.75PCZ, 1.0PCZ, and 1.25PCZ were prepared in the same way by mixing 0.75, 1.0, and 1.25 wt% of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles, respectively.

In addition, different thick polymer films were prepared by transferring known volume of 1 wt% of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles dispersed polymer solution into the stainless steel cup for curing. For example, 100 μm thick polymer film was obtained with ∼2 ml of 1 wt% of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles dispersed polymer solution. Similarly, ∼5, ∼7 and ∼10 ml of polymer solution was transferred into a stainless steel cup for making 150, 200, 250 μm thick polymer films, respectively. The prepared polymer films were labelled as PCZ-1 (100 μm), PCZ-2 (150 μm), PCZ-3(200 μm), and PCZ-4 (250 μm). In addition, a pure polymer film (without CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles) and polymer film with N-ZnO nanoparticles were prepared, for comparison.

The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were recorded by a Rigaku using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.1546 nm) monochromated by a nickel filter. Morphology was investigated by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, SUPRA-40VP equipped with an EDXS system) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL-2010). The particles size was measured by nanoparticle size analyzer (Nanophox, NX0070, Sympatec GmbH). Raman spectra were collected on Raman spectroscope (nano-Raman, NTEGRA) equipped with a synapse CCD detector and a confocal Olympus microscope. The surface electronic states were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS Ultra DLD, Kratos. Inc), monochromatic Al Kα radiation (1486.6 eV, 150 W). The optical property was analyzed with an UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu). The UV light absorption intensity was measured with help of UV light Digital meter (UV-340A, Lutron). Two different light sources, a UV lamp (18W, 365 nm) and direct daylight (Sunlight, 3–15th June 2017) were used to investigate the UV-shielding property.

Fig. 2 a shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles. All peaks in the pattern were indexed as the hexagonal phase ZnO (JCPDS No. 79–0206). The primary diffraction peaks appeared at 2θ values of 31.7°, 34.4° and 36.2°, representing (100), (002) and (101) assigned crystal planes of ZnO [29]. A graphitic (002) peak appeared near 26°, indicating the CQDs were covered on ZnO surface [28]. A similar XRD pattern was observed after dispersing different wt.% of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles in PMMA matrix, as shown in Fig. 2b. However, it was hard to find the graphic diffraction peak in the polymer film. In order to confirm the existence of CQDs on the surface of ZnO nanoparticles, nano-Raman spectroscope was recorded to check the Raman-active modes of CQD and ZnO in CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles [30]. The occurrence of carbon related Raman-active modes signifying the presence of CQD and ZnO phases in CQD/N-ZnO nanomaterials, other active modes were described in Fig S1 (in supplementary information file). In addition, the full survey X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) scan of CQD/N-ZnO nanomaterials supported the existence of Zn, O, C and N, as displayed in Fig. S2 (in supplementary information). The core level high resolution N 1s spectrum authenticated the N-doping with the occurrence of characteristic binding energies at 396.1 (-N-Zn-N-), 397.2 (-N-C-), 398.8 (-N-H-), and 400.1 eV (–O-Zn-N-) [31,32].

Fig. 3 depicts the HRTEM image and particle size analysis data of CQDs. Based on the HRTEM image, the average particle size of CQDs were noted to be ∼2.5 nm and the results was further confirmed using nanoparticle size analyzer, as shown in Fig. 3b. It was witnessed that the maximum percentage of particle size distribution at 2.6 nm. These observations confirm the size of CQDs were around 2.6 nm. Fig. 4 a shows the high magnified FESEM image of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles. Average particle size of N-ZnO nanoparticles was found to be ∼25 nm and it seems to be the spherical shaped nanoparticles that was further confirmed by TEM image, as shown in Fig. 4b. Nevertheless, it was tough to visualize the variation in particle size after CQDs coverage onto the ZnO crystals due to smaller particle size of CQDs. However, HRTEM image can helps to resolve existence of both phases of CQDs and ZnO in CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles. Fig. 4c–d reveal the HRTEM images of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles and the lattice spacing were measured to be ∼0.26 and ∼0.32 nm for ZnO and carbon, respectively [32]. The inset in Fig. 4b shows selected area electron diffraction pattern of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticle, which indicates the patterns exhibit lattice spacing and concentric rings due to the crystalline nature of the ZnO and CQDs [33]. These observations were strongly authenticating the existence of CQDs and ZnO phases in CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles.

Fig. 5 illustrates a simple experimental setup to measure the UV shielding capability of the polymer film. A UV digital lux meter was placed just below (3 cm) the polymer film holder. The light source may be of either a UV lamp or daylight. A digital lux UV meter was used to measure the UV light intensity after passing through the polymer film. The polymer films with increasing content of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles were measured under two different light sources and the results were displayed in Fig. 6 . A pure PMMA polymer film without CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles shields only ∼7% of UV light. The representative polymer film (0.5PCZ) containing 0.5 wt.% of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles blocked ∼22% under the illumination of a UV lamp. Likewise, other polymer films, 0.75PCZ, 1.0PCZ, 1.25PCZ and 1.5PCZ were absorbs UV light intensity of ∼29, 45, 47 and 53% of UV shielding capability, respectively as shown in Fig. 6a. A similar experiment was performed under daylight source and results were consistent, as shown in Fig. 6b. Based on these results, it was concluded that 1 wt.% of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles could be the optimum percentage for better UV shielding capability. This observation was further clarified with a UV–vis absorption spectrum of 1.0PCZ, as shown in Fig. 7 . It was detected that a strong absorption peak near UV spectral range confirms the UV light shielding capability of 1.0PCZ polymer film [20]. Besides, the photographs were taken after curing the polymer films as a function of increasing wt.% of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles, displayed as inset in Fig. 6. The photographic image clearly visualized that a good dispersion of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles throughout the polymer film and it was hard to find any micron level particle aggregation.

Shielding of 47% of UV light is not good enough to use as a UV shielding polymer film. Hence, to improve further, different thick polymer films were prepared with 1.0 wt.% CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles dispersed polymer solution. Fig. 8 shows the UV shielding capability of polymer films with respect to the increasing thickness under a UV lamp and daylight sources. The intensity of UV light from the direct daylight was recorded as 3230 μW/cm−2. The 100 μm thick polymer film containing 1.0 wt% of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles absorbs ∼62% (1220 μW/cm−2) from the daylight source. While, the same thickness PMMA polymer film absorbs just ∼8% (2960 μW/cm−2) of UV light and the maximum of 25% of UV shielding capability recorded in 250 μm thick PMMA polymer film under daylight source, as shown Fig. S3 (in electronic supplementary information file). This result expresses the importance of UV shielding characteristics of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles. Accordingly, the UV light absorption trend was increased by increasing the thickness of the polymer films, as shown in Fig. 8a. For instant, around 73% (840 μW/cm−2) of UV light was absorbed by PCZ-2 (150 μm) thick polymer film. However, a significant enhancement was observed in a polymer film, PCZ-4 (250 μm) with the maximum of ∼92% (252 μW/cm−2) of UV light shielding, under the direct daylight.

In parallel, a UV lamp was used to evaluate the UV light shielding capacity of different thick polymer films, as shown in Fig. 8b. The intensity of UV light recorded from a UV lamp was 752 μW/cm−2. The 100 μm thick polymer film absorbs ∼54% (354 μW/cm−2), while the UV light shielding capability of pristine PMMA polymer film of same thickness shielded ∼10% (710 μW/cm−2), as shown Fig. S4 (in electronic supplementary information file). Whereas, the 150 μm thick PCZ-2 and PCZ-4 with 250 μm thickness polymer films shielded ∼69% (230 μW/cm−2) and 84.5% (116 μW/cm−2), respectively. Based on these results, the substantial improvement in the UV shielding capability of the polymer film was realized upon increasing the thickness of the polymer film, irrespective to the light sources. This observation strongly authenticated that the polymer film thickness plays a vital role to improve the UV shielding characteristics of the polymer film. Although there are certain numbers of publication discussed that ZnO nanomaterials shield more than 90% of UV light, it was all about qualitative results measured from the UV–visible spectral data [16–18,34,35]. However, we report UV shielding capability of the polymer film based on the quantitative calculation with the help of a UV digital lux meter. The possible mechanism for higher UV shielding capacity of polymer film is mainly due to high level of light scattering and reflection exerted by CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles that can rapidly convert the photon energy of UV light into heat [3]. Besides, the transparency of the 250 μm thick polymer film (PCZ-4) was compared with pure PMMA polymer. The inset in Fig. 7 shows the photographic images of pure and 1.0PCZ polymer films, the transparency of the polymer films remains unaffected except a slight color shade. The other photographs of different thick polymer films (PCZ-1, PCZ-2 and PCZ-3) were displayed in Fig. S5 (in electronic supplementary information file), for comparison.

In order to realize the role of CQDs in polymer film, the comparative graph was drawn between the pristine PMMA, N-ZnO and PCZ-4 of same 250 μm thickness, as shown in Fig. 8c. UV shielding capability of pristine PMMA, N-ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles dispersed polymer films were calculated ∼25, 60 and 92%, respectively. More than 30% of UV light shielding between PCZ-4 and N-ZnO nanoparticles dispersed polymer films, was primarily due to the surface modification of CQDs, which facilitates easy dispersion in the polymer matrix. Such a synthetic strategy can greatly enhance the compatibility between the CQD/N-ZnO and polymer so as to keep high transparency. Therefore, a suitable, but not excessive amount of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles was responsible for the good dispersion in the polymer matrix, which significantly enhance the UV shielding capacity with an optimum thickness. Hence, it was concluded that the CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles dispersed polymer films could be a better alternative to protect UV light sensitive materials.

A simple method was demonstrated to obtained flexible CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles dispersed polymer film. The surface modification with CQDs can greatly enhance the compatibility between the CQD/N-ZnO and polymer so as to achieve high transparency of polymer films. The UV shielding characteristics was enhanced two-fold by changing thickness to 250 μm, instead of increasing the content of UV shielding materials. The maximum of ∼92% of UV light was shielded by a polymer film containing 1.0 wt.% of CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles having a thickness of 250 μm, under the direct daylight. Two different light sources were used to compare the UV shielding characteristic of the polymer films. Hence, it is foreseen that CQD/N-ZnO nanoparticles dispersed polymer films can be explored as potential candidate as UV light filter to protect UV light sensitive materials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was encouraged by the management of Sona College of Technology and supported by BK21 plus project in 2018, and National Research Foundation of Korea (KRF-2017R1A2B3006141).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2018.08.016.
